name: MIT License
description: A short and simple permissive license with conditions only requiring preservation of copyright and license notices.
conditions: License and copyright notice must be included
example: React, Vue, jQuery, and Express are licensed under MIT.

name: Apache License 2.0
description: A permissive license whose main conditions require preservation of copyright and license notices. Contributors provide an express grant of patent rights.
example: Kubernetes, Docker, and TensorFlow are licensed under Apache 2.0.

name: BSD 2-Clause License
description: A very simple permissive license that allows unlimited redistribution for any purpose as long as copyright notices and the license's disclaimers are maintained.
conditions: License and copyright notice must be included
example: FreeBSD, Nginx, and Sentry (parts) use BSD 2-Clause.

name: BSD 3-Clause License
description: A permissive license similar to the BSD 2-Clause but with a clause prohibiting the use of the name of the project or its contributors for endorsement without prior written permission.
conditions: License and copyright notice must be included
example: Go, FreeBSD, and NumPy are licensed under BSD 3-Clause.

name: ISC License
description: A highly permissive license functionally equivalent to the two-clause BSD license and MIT license, but with even simpler language, removing language deemed unnecessary by the Berne Convention.
conditions: License and copyright notice must be included
example: npm, OpenSSH, and Node.js (some core dependencies) use the ISC license.

name: zlib License
description: A permissive license designed for the zlib compression library. It allows software modification and distribution, provided that the origin is not misrepresented and modifications are marked.
conditions: Modified versions must be marked as such
Do not misrepresent the origin of the original software
example: zlib, LibPNG, and SFML are licensed under the zlib License.

name: Artistic License 2.0
description: A permissive license used primarily for Perl projects. It allows distribution and modification but requires modified packages to still be accessible, clearly documented, or released under compatible terms.
conditions: Maintain compatibility or document changes clearly
example: Perl 5, Raku (formerly Perl 6), and associated modules are licensed under the Artistic License.

name: Python Software Foundation License
description: A BSD-style permissive license designed for the Python programming language. It permits modification and commercial redistribution without requiring copyleft constraints.
conditions: License and copyright notice must be included
State changes in Python files if modified
example: The main Python implementation (CPython) is licensed under the PSFL.

name: Boost Software License 1.0
description: A highly permissive license similar to MIT and BSD, but unlike them, it does not require copyleft or attribution notices if the software is distributed in binary/compiled form.
conditions: License and copyright notice required only for source code distribution
example: The Boost C++ Libraries and the D programming language compiler are licensed under BSL 1.0.

name: University of Illinois/NCSA Open Source License
description: A permissive license combining terms from the MIT/X11 and BSD licenses, requiring license text preservation but prohibiting promotional use of developer names.
conditions: License and copyright notice must be included
Names of developers/institutes cannot be used for promotion
example: LLVM compiler infrastructure, Clang, and NCSA HTTPd were licensed under NCSA.

name: Mozilla Public License 2.0
description: A weak copyleft license that requires source code of modified files to be made available. However, files with new/different code can be under other licenses.
example: Firefox, Rust's cargo-edit, and Terraform are licensed under MPL 2.0.

name: GNU Lesser General Public License v2.1
description: A weak copyleft license allowing developers to link to a shared library without forcing their own application to be open sourced, though modifications to the library itself must remain under LGPL.
conditions: Allow users to relink or reverse engineer the host application
example: FFmpeg (standard config), GTK 2, and GNU C Library (glibc) are licensed under LGPL 2.1.

name: GNU Lesser General Public License v3.0
description: An updated version of LGPL v2.1 incorporating GPLv3's terms. It guarantees rights to relink and reverse-engineer, while offering patent grants and protection against anti-circumvention laws.
conditions: Allow users to relink or reverse engineer the host application
example: 7-Zip (some modules), Mono Class Libraries, and GNU MPFR are licensed under LGPL 3.0.

name: Eclipse Public License 2.0
description: A weak copyleft license designed to be business-friendly. Modified files must be disclosed, but it allows sub-licensing of binaries and provides clear patent terms.
conditions: Disclose source of modified EPL files
example: Eclipse IDE, Jetty web server, and Clojure are licensed under EPL 2.0.

name: Common Development and Distribution License
description: A weak copyleft license based on the Mozilla Public License. It requires source code release of CDDL-covered files if modified, but allows combining them with other proprietary files.
example: OpenSolaris, ZFS (OpenZFS), and GlassFish are licensed under CDDL.

name: GNU General Public License v2.0
description: A strong copyleft license that requires any derivative work, linked code, or modifications to be distributed under the exact same license terms. Source code must be provided.
example: The Linux Kernel, Git (v2 only), and MySQL (community) are licensed under GPLv2.

name: GNU GPLv3
description: A strong copyleft license requiring derivative works to be open sourced under GPLv3. It provides explicit protection against patent litigation and tivoization (hardware locking source code).
example: Bash, GnuPG, and GCC are licensed under GPLv3.

name: GNU AGPLv3
description: A strong copyleft license designed for network-served software. If you run a modified version on a server for users to interact with over a network, you must disclose the source code.
conditions: Network trigger (disclose source when run on a network)
example: Mastodon, Grafana, and MongoDB (previously) used AGPLv3.

name: European Union Public Licence 1.2
description: A strong copyleft license drafted in 22 European languages, conforming to EU law. It has compatibility provisions allowing code to be integrated with GPL, MPL, and other copyleft licenses.
example: Joint Research Centre (JRC) software and various EU government tools use EUPL.

name: CeCILL License v2.1
description: A French law-compliant strong copyleft license designed by CEA, CNRS, and Inria. It is fully compatible with GNU GPL, facilitating copyleft licensing within European/French legal frameworks.
example: Gnuplot (partially), French academic software projects use CeCILL.

name: The Unlicense
description: A public domain equivalent license that releases the code into the public domain, allowing anyone to do whatever they want with it, without any conditions.
example: youtube-dl and sqlite-jdbc are licensed under Unlicense.

name: CC0 1.0 Universal
description: A public dedication tool from Creative Commons that waives all copyright and related rights in a work worldwide, to the fullest extent permitted by law.
example: SQLite, public domain assets, and components of various open repositories use CC0.
